title: Five (Nearly) Effortlessly Fun Games
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZE9ENcHPRr8

Daniel (@dungeondive) builds a whole list around his "effortless fun" idea — games that get as close as a board game can to frictionless play, judged on setup/teardown, ease of learning, length, table footprint, thematic punch, and how much he simply enjoys them. He opens by polling patrons (whose wildly varied answers — Mage Knight as "effortless"?! — prove how subjective the category is), then counts down five picks in box-size order.

His five: Seven Moons: Heroes of Dragonreach (a small-box Runebound-lite with a lovely hex map); Escape the Dark Castle (a deck-of-cards Fighting Fantasy, perfect as a party nightcap); Glory 2nd Edition (a stripped-down Talisman/Runebound, d8-only, six-page rulebook); Bin1 (Paul Stapleton's delightful *urban* adventure game set in Brighton, a rare non-fantasy entry); and Lands of Galzyr (his beloved open-world critter-crawl sandbox). Throughout he's emphatic that "effortless" doesn't mean "baby game" — he's played these since the mid-80s and prizes simpler designs more than most channels. Recurring wishes: small card-only expansions adding quest/encounter variety to Seven Moons and Glory. A warm, opinionated tour of low-friction adventure gaming.

----

Daniel insists "effortless fun" doesn't mean "baby game" — he prizes simple designs after 40 years in the hobby. What's a deceptively simple game you rate far higher than its rules-weight would suggest?
